Email LinkedIn Instagram
 

I’m a graphically-inclined designer intrigued by the synthesis of multidimensional interoperability; 3D elements in 2D spaces. I navigate product visuals through the identification and construction of meaningful narratives.


Album Campaign

How might we redesign album imagery and materials to better serve the music's tone and message?

Course:
DES 335: Images in Communication

Semester:
Fall 2025

Instructor:
Carley Law

Tools Used: Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Illustrator, Blender



Brainstorming and Problem Identification

My initial brainstorming sought to identify aspects of the original album materials that worked really well and how I might adapt them. I really wanted to avoid straying too far from the identity that King Princess (the artist) had created while still improving upon the work.

The problem I found with the original album materials was that their vibrant, colorful nature- paired with the cartoon cherry- didn't feel like a match for the music's sound. For the gritty message of heartbreak and disassociation, the visuals should maintain a similar amount of grit.

The Cover

The cover recieved the most amount of my time. I knew I would be using pieces from it in other parts of the campaign, so I wanted to ensure it's visuals and identity were as strong as possible.

I employed multimedia compositional techniques to create the cherry's splatter and glass shattering effect. I took a screenshot from this great video of one of KP’s performances and modified it to work for my cover.


For the gatefold’s interior, I used a photo taken by Naina Srivastava from ACL in place of my own (due to some issues regarding storage space, they were lost to time).

Additional Assets

The remaining assets were important to complete to make the campaign feel complete. These were created using design motifs and assets from the album cover.

Poster

Booklet

Record Label

VIP Pass

Renders

Originally, these materials were arranged and shot physically. Without quick access to a lightbooth, however, the quality didn't meet my standards. To rectify this, I recreated the assets in Blender and rendered them out.

Takeaways

- As my largest project using primarily Photoshop, I was suprised by just how powerful the software is.

- I feel like I have a much better understanding of the processes required to created a fully fleshed set of materials digitally.

- If I had corrospondence with the artist, I would've loved to iterate upon these materials and create a deliverable that satisfied them.

CENTENNIAL

RPG video game players should be able to discern game aesthetic and mechanics through story-backed typography,gameplay user interface, and graphics decisions.

Class:
Typography and Games

Semester:
Spring 2026

Instructors:
Kelcey Gray and Jessie Contour

Tools Used: Adobe Photoshop, Adobe Illustrator, Blender, Calligraphr



Role

As an independent project, I was responsible for everything that needed to go into the final product: collecting, adapting, and importing open source assets, brainstorming and writing character profiles, and the entirety of the technical artist pipeline (from sketch to model to animated rig). This also includes (and was one of the primary deliverables of the assignment) learning new softwares and techniques- especially Unity.


Phase 1: Sketching, Stories, and Ideation

What is CENTENNIAL?

Our instructors gave us a matrix and Mad Libs style prompt to encourage us to think deeply about what our game concepts would be, who they would be for, and what they could look like. Below is my resulting description:

CENTENNIAL is a singleplayer Strategy/Action-RPG game for individuals aged 17 and up. CENTENNIAL pushes players into a gripping sci-fi political domination setting with its painterly dystopia. Players are put into the shoes of chancellors on a generation ship and must recapture zones from a rogue AI at large while maintaining public approval. Choose your next move wisely, as a couple wrong turns could tank your rating and cast the ship into irreparable disarray.

I knew going into this I would need to manage my expectations with regards to what I would be able to accomplish. Learning things takes time, and implementing the things I would be learning simultaneously would require even more.

I began with something I’m more familiar with: digital illustration and constructing character narratives. This gave me a solid foundation to begin working on some of the aspects of the project that were more foreign to me.

Concepts for a few playable chancellors. Their designs are indicative of their personality, platform, and potential playstyle.

Concepts for the ship itself. I wanted an imposing, brick-like silhouette that gave off a dystopian vibe.

Concepts for a few enemies. Each enemy needed a function on the ship aside from being obstacles for players to beat the crap out of, so I designed with intention. For example, Epson is a large-scale laser printer that focuses beams of light into his prism to prime walls for inking. Punch-tron is also a printer (see below for more details).

It was also during this time that I began to come up with ideas for UI elements, like the game's logotype and typeface. I wanted each element to have a place in the game's universe. For example, the first font I made (dubbed 'Punch Gun') was a modular typeface that ship engineers and logistics crew would use to label boxes. The modularity comes from the circular form that all of the letters are constructed from, almost as if these workers had a 'punch gun' with tabs on its front to stamp the letters onto various surfaces quickly.




Phase 2: Feedback, Iteration, and Blender

Peer and instructor feedback was crucial in the improvement of the typeface I was creating. The first version of Punch Gun had very poor legibility, even for a display font. Letterforms weren't easily distinguishable from each other, which I needed to fix.

The tweaks I made included:

  • > Further subdividing the circle to allow for more variation in forms

  • > Increasing the width of the columns and rows of the circle

  • > 'Doubling up' on some letters, such as M, W, and K

For the logotype, I chose my favorite aspects from the concepts I drew and recreated them in Illustrator. I then imported the vectors as .svg files into Blender to extrude forms, apply materials, and add lighting. I'd use this method for a number of other assets as well.

My blender viewport during this part of asset creation.

The character from the intial round of concept sketches that resonated with me the most was the first one, who I later named 'Chancellor Ironhand'. In game, she would be a close-quarters brawler with a focus on flat damage and mobility. I drew inspiration from US military ceremonial garb as I rendered out her character portrait in Photoshop, using the sketch from before (I also began work on a turnaround, but found that the portrait was sufficient reference):

To better distinguish my project from those of my peers, who were mainly operating in 2D, I decided to create my game in 3D. I planned to learn to create a functioning character controller, cloth physics, and rigged model. Below are just a few screenshots from the hours I spent developing these skills:

This process is where I really began to feel the time crunch. I had to make executive decisions about the 3D pipeline and level of detail I wanted to achieve. Ideally, I would spend far longer on the model alone; sculpting, retopologizing, and baking details. This project didn't offer the timeframe that such an involved process requires, so I opted instead for a quicker route- poly modeling with flat textures.

Other assets made during this phase

The player's health bar and ability display

The ship itself, made with a free procedural greeble texture

Phase 3: Unity, Exporting, and Publishing

After hours alone spent troubleshooting .fbx uploads to Unity, sourcing a number of assets from the web (such as an HDR, level map, and mixamo mocap animations), and finagling with lighting, I had a final product that met the intial project requirements and then some.

The project is available to play on itch.io.

Takeaways

- Time is something to be budgeted. My time management skills were improved significantly from this project alone; I had to delegate based on quality over achievability at several points.

- My understanding of the technical artist pipeline has increased exponentially; I feel prepared to tackle larger, more detailed projects with different methods (I'm desperately trying to find the time to finish a project with sculpting and retopology).

- I want to continue working on this project in the future; getting it to a true playable state would be artistically fulfilling.

LHD Logo Redesign

Longhorn Developers, as an organization, should be able to freely use and display their logo iconography to promote unity and togetherness among their members.

Client:
Longhorn Developers

Semester:
Fall 2025

Role:
Designer

Tools Used:Adobe Illustrator, Blender



Problem Identification and Ideation

LHD's leadership was looking to expand their brand, but with their previous logo (which utilized the iconic apple 'rock hand' emoji) they were limited in what they could and couldn't use it on. They sent out a call for help to the newly introduced design team to submit ideas for what their new logo could be. As one of these design team members, I was excited to throw my hat in the ring.

After looking into the logos of thematically relevant companies and softwares, such as Github, StackOverflow, and Bitbucket, I drew up these few ideas and sent it over. I knew the turnaround was going to be very quick, so I only made a few sketches.

Iterating

The leadership favored the 'pixel hook'em' option began to work with me regarding choices on shapes, color, edge rounding, etc.

After these rounds of design, the vectors were imported to figma so that the whole team could tinker with the forms. Below are some of our group iterations:

And this is how it turned out:




Takeaways

- This project tested the efficiency of my design process. With a quick turnaround and a lot of back and forth dialogue, I had to be able to quickly iterate and make decisions.

- Despite the atypical circumstances and fast-paced nature of this project, I feel satisfied with the outcome and am glad that the organization is too.

- The logo is in use in a number of different places, most notably as the icon next to each member's position on LinkedIn and the org's instagram profile picture. In the future, it may be used on org merch and other physical materials.

Konstruct

Can a modular slab-serif typeface embody modernity?

Course:
DES 335: Typography II

Semester:
Spring 2026

Instructor:
Carley Law

Tools Used: Adobe Illustrator, Blender



this project is currently ongoing and thus the case study is still under construction, sorry!
